sound.title: Sound
keyQuestions: How is sound produced?
What is vibration?
What determines the pitch of a sound?
What determines the loudness of a sound?
Can sound travel through a vacuum? Why?
What is an echo and how is it produced?
keyIdeas: Sound is produced when an object vibrates. The vibration causes particles of the surrounding medium to vibrate, transmitting the sound.
Sound needs a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel. Sound cannot travel through a vacuum because there are no particles to vibrate.
The pitch of a sound is determined by the frequency of vibration: faster vibration gives a higher pitch.
The loudness of a sound is determined by the amplitude of vibration: larger vibration gives a louder sound.
An echo is a reflected sound, heard after the original sound when sound waves bounce off a hard, flat surface.

glossary.definition: A form of energy produced by vibrating objects. Travels as waves through a medium (solid, liquid, or gas). Cannot travel through a vacuum.
A rapid back-and-forth movement of an object. All sound is caused by vibration. When vibrations stop, sound stops.
The substance through which sound waves travel (solid, liquid, or gas). Sound cannot travel through a vacuum (empty space).
How high or low a sound is. Determined by the frequency (rate) of vibration. Fast vibration = high pitch; slow vibration = low pitch.
How loud or soft a sound is. Determined by the amplitude (size) of the vibration. Large vibration = loud sound; small vibration = soft sound.
The number of vibrations per second. Measured in hertz (Hz). High frequency = high pitch.
The size (height) of a sound wave. Large amplitude = loud sound. Small amplitude = soft sound.
A reflected sound heard after the original sound. Occurs when sound bounces off a hard, flat surface (e.g. a cliff or wall).
A space with no matter (no particles). Sound cannot travel through a vacuum because there are no particles to vibrate.
A region in a sound wave where particles are pushed close together (high pressure).
A region in a sound wave where particles are spread apart (low pressure). Alternates with compressions.

misconceptions.claim: "Sound can travel through a vacuum; you just need to be very loud."
misconceptions.correction: Sound CANNOT travel through a vacuum at any volume. It needs particles (solid, liquid, or gas) to vibrate. In space, no one hears the explosion.

misconceptions.claim: "Pitch and loudness are the same thing."
misconceptions.correction: Pitch is how high or low (frequency). Loudness is how soft or loud (amplitude). Fast vibration = high pitch; big vibration = loud sound. They are independent.

misconceptions.claim: "Sound travels faster in air than in water or solids."
misconceptions.correction: Sound travels FASTER in solids and liquids than in gases, because the particles are closer together. In a solid like steel, sound is roughly 15 times faster than in air.

light.title: Light & Shadows
keyQuestions: How does light travel?
How is a shadow formed?
What affects the size of a shadow?
What is the difference between transparent, translucent and opaque?
What happens to light when it hits a mirror?
keyIdeas: Light travels in straight lines. When light hits a surface, it can be reflected, absorbed, or transmitted, depending on the material.
A shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light. The shape of a shadow depends on the shape of the object, and the size depends on how close the object is to the light source.

glossary.definition: An object that produces its own light. E.g. the Sun, a lamp, a candle, fireflies. Objects that only reflect light (Moon, mirror) are NOT light sources.
Allows almost all light to pass through. Objects can be seen clearly. E.g. clear glass, clean water, clear plastic wrap.
glossary.template: A material that allows ___ light to pass through, and objects can be ___.

glossary.definition: Allows some light to pass through, but scatters it. Objects appear blurry through it. E.g. frosted glass, tissue paper, wax paper.
glossary.template: A material that allows ___ light to pass through, but objects cannot be ___.

glossary.definition: Blocks all light. No light passes through. Forms a clear shadow. E.g. wood, brick, cardboard, metal, your hand.
glossary.template: A material that does not allow ___ to pass through.

glossary.definition: A dark area formed when an opaque object blocks light. The shadow is always on the opposite side of the object from the light source.
Light bouncing off a surface. Smooth, shiny surfaces (mirrors, still water) reflect light clearly. Rough surfaces scatter it.
A straight-line representation of how light travels. Light always travels in straight lines unless it hits a new medium.

misconceptions.correction: The Moon only REFLECTS sunlight. It is not a light source. Real light sources produce their own light (Sun, flame, bulb, firefly).

misconceptions.claim: "Shadows get bigger when the object moves away from the light."
misconceptions.correction: Shadows get SMALLER when the object moves AWAY from the light, and BIGGER when it moves TOWARDS the light (because the light spreads out around it more).
Translucent materials let some light through but SCATTER it, so you cannot see objects clearly (frosted glass). Transparent lets light through without scattering.

heat.title: Heat & Temperature
keyQuestions: What is the difference between heat and temperature?
How does heat travel through a solid?
Why are metals good conductors of heat?
What is a convection current and how does it form?
Can heat travel through empty space? How?
Which surfaces absorb radiation best?
What happens when two objects reach the same temperature?
Why do railway tracks have gaps between them?
How does a thermometer measure temperature?
What is the difference between melting and boiling?
keyIdeas: Heat is a form of energy. Heat always flows from a hotter object to a colder object, until both reach the same temperature.
Heat can be transferred in three ways: by conduction (through solids), by convection (through liquids and gases), and by radiation (through empty space).
Heat has observable effects: it causes materials to expand when heated and contract when cooled, and it can change the state of matter.

glossary.definition: A form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a cooler object. Measured in joules (J). Heat is not the same as temperature.
glossary.template: A form of energy that flows from ___ to ___.

glossary.definition: A measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of an object. Measured in degrees Celsius (°C) using a thermometer. It does not depend on the amount of substance.
glossary.template: A measure of how ___ or ___ something is.

glossary.definition: The transfer of heat through direct contact between particles. Heat moves from hotter to cooler regions. Common in solids, especially metals.
A material that allows heat to pass through it easily. Metals such as copper, aluminium, and iron are good conductors. Copper conducts best.
A material that does not allow heat to pass through it easily. Examples include wood, plastic, rubber, air, wool, and glass. Used to reduce heat loss.
The transfer of heat through a fluid (liquid or gas) by the movement of the fluid itself. Hot fluid rises; cool fluid sinks, forming a convection current.
The circular movement of a fluid caused by differences in density due to temperature. Warm fluid rises (less dense) and cool fluid sinks (more dense).
The transfer of heat energy as electromagnetic waves (infrared radiation). It does not require a medium — it can travel through a vacuum. The Sun heats Earth by radiation.
The state reached when two objects in contact have the same temperature and heat transfer between them stops.
The increase in size of a material when its temperature rises. Particles move faster and further apart. Most solids, liquids, and gases expand when heated.
The temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid. For ice (water), the melting point is 0°C.
The temperature at which a liquid changes to a gas (vapour) throughout its entire volume. For water, the boiling point is 100°C.

misconceptions.claim: "Heat and temperature are the same thing."
misconceptions.correction: Heat is a form of energy (joules). Temperature measures how hot or cold (°C). A bathtub of warm water has more heat energy than a boiling cup, but a lower temperature.
Both are at room temperature when they have been in the room. Metal FEELS colder because it conducts heat away from your hand faster. It is not actually colder.
Convection happens in liquids AND gases (fluids). It is why warm air rises in a room, not just why warm water rises in a pot.

energy-conversion.title: Energy & Energy Conversion
keyQuestions: What is energy and what forms can it take?
What does it mean to say energy is 'converted'?
Why is some of the input energy always 'wasted'?
What is the law of conservation of energy?
Where does the energy in our food originally come from?
How is a device like a torch or a kettle an 'energy converter'?
Why do moving parts get hot?
keyIdeas: Energy is the ability to do work. It exists in many forms: kinetic (motion), potential (stored), heat, light, sound, electrical, chemical, and nuclear.
Potential energy can be gravitational (due to height) or elastic (due to being stretched or compressed).
Energy is never created or destroyed; it is only converted from one form to another. This is the law of conservation of energy.
Every energy converter produces useful output energy AND wasted output energy. Wasted energy is usually in the form of heat, and sometimes sound.
The Sun is the main source of energy on Earth. Almost every food chain traces back to the Sun via photosynthesis.
Renewable sources (Sun, wind, moving water) can be replenished within a human lifetime. Non-renewable sources (coal, oil, gas) cannot.

Common conversions: chemical → electrical (battery), electrical → light + heat (bulb), electrical → kinetic + heat + sound (motor), kinetic → electrical (generator, dynamo), light → chemical (photosynthesis), light → electrical (solar cell), chemical → kinetic + heat (muscles), potential → kinetic (falling object).
Energy is the ability to do work. It exists in many forms: light, heat, sound, electrical, kinetic (motion), and potential (stored).
Potential energy can be gravitational (due to an object's height above the ground) or elastic (stored in a stretched or compressed object).
Energy can be converted from one form to another, but it cannot be created or destroyed.
The Sun is the main source of energy on Earth. Energy from the Sun drives photosynthesis, the water cycle, and weather.
Renewable energy sources (Sun, wind, moving water) can be replaced naturally. Non-renewable sources (coal, oil, gas) cannot be replaced within a human lifetime.

glossary.definition: The ability to do work. Measured in joules (J). Energy exists in many forms and can be converted from one form to another.
glossary.template: The ability to ___.

glossary.definition: The energy of a moving object. A fast car has more kinetic energy than a slow one; a heavy lorry has more kinetic energy than a light car at the same speed.
glossary.template: The energy of ___.

glossary.definition: Stored energy due to an object's position or condition. Two main types: gravitational (due to height) and elastic (due to being stretched or compressed).
glossary.template: Energy stored in an object due to its ___ or ___.

glossary.definition: Energy stored in an object because of its height above the ground. A ball held up high has gravitational potential energy; when released, it is converted to kinetic energy as the ball falls.
glossary.template: Energy stored in an object due to its ___ above the ground.

glossary.definition: Energy stored in an object that has been stretched or compressed (e.g. a stretched elastic band, a compressed spring). Released when the object returns to its original shape.
glossary.template: Energy stored in a ___ or ___ object.

glossary.definition: A form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a cooler one. Usually the 'wasted' output of energy converters (e.g. a bulb gets hot, a motor gets warm).
glossary.template: The form of energy that flows from a ___ object to a ___ object.

glossary.definition: A form of energy that lets us see. Given off by the Sun, flames, and hot or excited atoms (bulbs, LEDs).
glossary.template: The form of energy that lets us ___.

glossary.definition: A form of energy produced by vibrating objects. Travels through a medium as waves. Usually a small, wasted output of energy converters (the hum of a fan, the click of a switch).
glossary.template: A form of energy produced by ___ objects.

glossary.definition: Energy carried by moving electric charges. Carried through wires from source (battery or plug) to device. Easily converted to other forms.
glossary.template: Energy carried by moving ___ ___.

glossary.definition: Energy stored in the bonds between atoms in food, fuel, and batteries. Released when the bonds are broken (e.g. when food is respired, when a battery is used).
glossary.template: Energy stored in the ___ of food, fuel, or batteries.

glossary.definition: Changing energy from one form to another. No energy is destroyed in a conversion; it just changes form. Useful + wasted outputs together equal the input.
glossary.template: Changing energy from ___ form to ___.

glossary.definition: A device that changes one form of energy into another. Examples: a bulb (electrical → light + heat), a solar cell (light → electrical), a battery (chemical → electrical), a muscle (chemical → kinetic + heat).
glossary.template: A device that changes ___ form of energy into ___.

glossary.definition: The output energy form we want from a converter. For a bulb it is light; for a motor it is kinetic energy; for a loudspeaker it is sound.
glossary.template: The output energy form we ___.

glossary.definition: The output energy form we do NOT want. Almost always includes heat, and sometimes sound. Wasted energy is not 'lost' — it spreads out into the surroundings.
glossary.template: The output energy form we do ___.

glossary.definition: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; it can only be converted from one form to another. The total energy before = total energy after.
glossary.template: Energy cannot be ___ or ___; only ___.

glossary.definition: An energy source that can be replaced naturally within a short time. Examples: solar, wind, moving water, geothermal, biomass.
glossary.template: An energy source that can be ___ naturally.

glossary.definition: An energy source that cannot be replaced within a human lifetime. Examples: coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear fuel. Using them contributes to pollution and climate change.
glossary.template: An energy source that cannot be ___ within a human lifetime.

pslePrompts: When asked about an energy conversion, state BOTH the useful output AND the wasted output. Bulbs are not just light — they are light + heat.
Use the phrasing 'converted from X energy to Y energy', not 'changed'.
For photosynthesis: light energy → chemical energy (stored in food).
For a falling object: gravitational potential energy → kinetic energy (and a small amount of heat and sound on impact).

questions.stem: A roller coaster car is pulled to the top of the first hill. It is then released and rolls down. Describe the energy changes that take place from the top of the hill to the bottom.
questions.modelAnswer: At the top of the hill, the car has maximum gravitational potential energy and minimum kinetic energy. As the car rolls down, its height decreases, so gravitational potential energy decreases. At the same time, the car moves faster, so kinetic energy increases. At the bottom of the hill, the car has minimum gravitational potential energy and maximum kinetic energy.

questions.stem: Alexey touched a metal spoon and a wooden spoon, both at the same room temperature. He said the metal spoon felt colder. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: The metal spoon and the wooden spoon are both at room temperature, which is lower than Alexey's body temperature. Metal is a good conductor of heat, so it conducts heat away from Alexey's hand quickly, making the spoon feel cold. Wood is a poor conductor of heat, so it does not conduct heat away as quickly, and therefore feels less cold.

questions.stem: When a pot of water is heated on a stove, bubbles form at the bottom of the pot and rise to the surface. Explain how the bubbles form and why they rise.
questions.modelAnswer: When the water at the bottom of the pot is heated, it gains enough heat to reach its boiling point. The water changes from a liquid to a gas (water vapour), forming bubbles. The bubbles rise to the surface because water vapour is less dense than liquid water.

questions.stem: Alexey noticed that his shadow is longer in the late afternoon than at noon. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: The size of a shadow depends on the angle of the light source. At noon, the Sun is high in the sky, almost overhead, so the light hits Alexey from a steep angle and his shadow is short. In the late afternoon, the Sun is lower in the sky, so the light hits him from a low angle, casting a longer shadow on the ground.

questions.stem: Why are pots and pans often made with metal bodies and plastic handles?
questions.modelAnswer: The body of the pot needs to transfer heat quickly from the stove to the food. Metal is a good conductor of heat, so it allows the food to be cooked quickly. The handle needs to stay cool enough to hold safely. Plastic is a poor conductor of heat (an insulator), so heat does not pass easily from the pot to the hand, and the handle stays safe to hold.

phenomena: A torch gets warm after being left on for several minutes.
A solar panel on a roof powers the house during the day.
A pendulum swings back and forth, gradually slowing down.
Rubbing two dry sticks together fast enough can start a fire.
A plant on a sunny windowsill grows taller over the week.

misconceptions.claim: "A bulb only converts electrical energy into light."
misconceptions.correction: A bulb converts electrical energy into LIGHT PLUS HEAT. The heat is the wasted output; you feel it when a bulb has been on for a while. PSLE answers must state both.
Wasted energy is NOT destroyed. It spreads out into the surroundings, usually as heat. The total energy before and after a conversion is the same (law of conservation of energy).

misconceptions.claim: "Renewable energy is the same as clean energy."
misconceptions.correction: Renewable means the source is replenished naturally (sun, wind, water). Most renewables are also clean, but biomass (wood) is renewable and still produces smoke when burned.
